name: quality-focused
description: Optimize for code quality: reduce complexity, improve naming, eliminate code smells, strengthen type safety

eval-lint.justification: Lint rules enforce consistent style and catch common defects. Weight reflects that linting is necessary but not sufficient for quality — it catches surface issues (naming, unused vars) but misses deeper structural problems.
eval-lint.reference: ISO/IEC 25010:2011 Section 4.2.6; Beller et al. 'Analyzing the State of Static Analysis' (ICSE 2016)

eval-types.justification: Static type checking prevents type-related runtime errors (null dereference, wrong argument types). Lower weight than tests because types catch a narrower class of defects, but higher confidence per finding.
eval-types.reference: ISO/IEC 25010:2011 Section 4.2.2; Gao et al. 'To Type or Not to Type' (ICSE 2017)

eval-tests.justification: Test pass rate measures behavioral correctness — whether the code does what it should. Equal weight to lint because both are essential: lint catches structural issues, tests catch behavioral ones.
eval-tests.reference: ISO/IEC 25010:2011 Section 4.2.2; Mockus et al. 'Test Coverage and Post-Verification Defects' (ESEM 2009)

eval-llm.justification: LLM rubric evaluates subjective quality dimensions (readability, architecture, idiomaticness) that automated tools miss. Highest weight because these dimensions have the strongest correlation with long-term maintainability in empirical studies.
eval-llm.reference: ISO/IEC 25010:2011 Section 4.2.6; Boehm et al. 'Quantitative Evaluation of Software Quality' (ICSE 1976)
